title: assertWorkPackageDeletePermission is imported by tests but does not exist
description: __tests__/api/work-package-permissions.unit.test.ts imports `assertWorkPackageDeletePermission` from `@/lib/auth/workPackage` (line 39) and runs a full describe block (`assertWorkPackageDeletePermission`) at line 108 with 6 test cases including missing-id 400, missing-WP 404, not-a-member 403, edit-only-no-delete 403, happy-path, and admin-bypass. The helper does not exist in the source — only `assertWorkPackageViewPermission` and `assertWorkPackageEditPermission` are exported (lines 27 and 55). The test file's import will fail at module load time, so the entire work-package-permissions suite cannot run. Additionally, the work-package `[id]` DELETE handler (work-packages/[id].ts:269) does an inline permission check using the SCREAMING_CASE `WORK_PACKAGE_DELETE` permission — the helper that test claims to exercise has not been implemented, and the in-handler check is the only place this is enforced.
suggested_fix: Either (a) add `assertWorkPackageDeletePermission(workPackageId, userId, isSystemAdmin)` to `lib/auth/workPackage.ts` (mirroring assertWorkPackageEditPermission but checking `WORK_PACKAGE_DELETE` in the role.permissions array), and refactor the DELETE branch of `work-packages/[id].ts` to call it, or (b) drop the test describe and import. The test file's intent suggests (a) is the right fix. Also add coverage for the orphan permission string so the route uses the helper consistently with the rest of Phase 7.

title: Three competing RBAC systems with no single source of truth
description: Phase 7 ships three parallel permission libraries: (1) `lib/auth/project.ts` — `assertProjectMembership` and `assertXxxProjectMembership` helpers that gate on Membership existence only; (2) `lib/permissions/check.ts` — `checkProjectPermission` / `requireProjectPermission` that gate on Membership + `Role.permissions: String[]` with `'*'` wildcard; (3) `lib/auth/workPackage.ts` — `assertWorkPackageViewPermission` / `assertWorkPackageEditPermission` that gate on Membership + SCREAMING_CASE `WORK_PACKAGE_*` strings. The split is visible in the routes themselves: `forums/[id]/index.ts` uses (1), `projects/[projectId]/wiki/index.ts` uses (2) via `requireProjectPermission`, and `work-packages/[id]/relations.ts` uses (3). A new Phase 7 dev has to learn three different code paths and the permission-string namespaces don't even match (`wiki.view` vs no equivalent for `WIKI_VIEW`). Spec §4.4 only defines the dot.notation names in (2). System (1) can't check fine-grained permissions at all — it just returns 403/404, so the helper has to be called once per route to even know what permission is needed.
suggested_fix: Pick one. Easiest path: make `assertXxxProjectMembership` accept an optional `permission?: ProjectPermission` arg and delegate to `checkProjectPermission` (which already handles the wildcard + membership lookup). Then deprecate the SCREAMING_CASE path in `lib/auth/workPackage.ts` by mapping `WORK_PACKAGE_EDIT` → `work_packages.edit` in the seed and removing (3). Mark `lib/permissions/check.ts` as the canonical home; re-export `assertProjectMembership` from `lib/auth/project.ts` as a thin shim. Add a test (`__tests__/lib/auth/project.unit.test.ts`) that exercises both the membership-only and membership+permission modes.

title: No unit test file for lib/auth/project.ts
description: This is the single most important Phase 7 file — 13 exported `assertXxxProjectMembership` helpers plus the base `assertProjectMembership`. The test directory has `__tests__/api/work-package-permissions.unit.test.ts` for the sibling `lib/auth/workPackage.ts`, and per-helper test files for documents, meetings, forums, wiki, relations, and work-packages-watch, but NO `__tests__/lib/auth/project.unit.test.ts`. The 80 unit tests cited in the AGENTS.md status line cover the *route* behaviour that uses these helpers, not the helpers themselves. Any refactor of the helpers (e.g. the wildcard-aware fix proposed in RBAC-2) would not have a failing test to catch regressions in the most-used RBAC surface in the codebase.
suggested_fix: Create `__tests__/lib/auth/project.unit.test.ts` mirroring the work-package-permissions test pattern (`vi.hoisted` mock factory for prisma). Cover at minimum: (a) `assertProjectMembership` — happy path, non-member 403, missing project 404, missing projectId 400, isSystemAdmin bypass; (b) `assertForumProjectMembership` — resolves forum → project, member happy, non-member 403, missing forum 404; (c) `assertThreadProjectMembership` — resolves thread → forum → project, returns forumId+projectId; (d) `assertPostProjectMembership` — full chain resolution; (e) `assertRelationProjectMembership` — resolves relation.from.projectId; (f) `assertWikiPageBySlugProjectMembership` — slug-ambiguity caveat (multiple projects with same slug). Aim for ≥40 unit tests on the helpers themselves.

title: assertWikiPageBySlugProjectMembership uses findFirst which is ambiguous across projects
description: Schema defines `WikiPage @@unique([projectId, slug])` (line 530 of schema.prisma) — i.e. a slug is unique per project, not globally. The helper does `prisma.wikiPage.findFirst({ where: { slug }, ... })` which returns the first matching page across all projects. If a user is a member of project A but the first match is in project B (different tenant), the helper incorrectly returns project B's pageId, returns 200 on the assertion, and the route then does another `findFirst({ where: { slug } })` returning the same wrong project. The code comment on line 263-267 acknowledges the ambiguity ('if the slug is ambiguous across projects the first match is used and the caller should also pass projectId via query to disambiguate') but the caller in `pages/api/wiki/by-slug/index.ts` doesn't do that — it only passes `slug` as a query param. The comment also says 'the URL route only carries `slug`' but the route only validates `slug` in querySchema.
suggested_fix: Either (a) require `projectId` in the helper signature and the route's `querySchema` (change `querySchema` to `z.object({ slug: z.string(), projectId: z.string().cuid() })` and call `assertWikiPageProjectMembership` with the resolved pageId), or (b) make `assertWikiPageBySlugProjectMembership` accept an optional `projectId?: string` and disambiguate the findFirst: if projectId is given, add `where: { slug, projectId }`; if not, throw a 400 requiring it. Option (a) is the safer fix — slugs are project-scoped per the schema unique constraint, and disambiguating at the API boundary matches the data model.

title: GET /api/work-packages/[id] has no auth or membership check
description: The route file comment at line 1-6 admits 'GET remains public-readable (project visibility is enforced elsewhere)'. There is no `assertWorkPackageViewPermission` call inside `getWorkPackage` (lines 72-97). An unauthenticated caller can fetch any work package's full payload — including the project record, status, type, priority, assignee email + avatar, author email + avatar, parent, and children — by ID enumeration. This contradicts the Sprint A1/2 hardening pattern applied to `/api/work-packages/[id]/activities`, `/relations`, `/time-entries`, and the `watch` endpoint, all of which call `assertWorkPackageViewPermission` in their own handler. The route is also not on `withRoute` HOF — it's a raw handler with manual rate-limit and auth on PATCH/DELETE only.
suggested_fix: Migrate the file to `withRoute` HOF (the TODO at line 6 already calls this out) and call `await assertWorkPackageViewPermission(id, session.user.id, !!session.user.isSystemAdmin)` at the top of every method. This will return 401 anonymous and 403 non-members, matching the rest of the work-package surface. The minimal non-migrating fix is to inline the check in `getWorkPackage` (lines 72-97).

title: Time-entry approve endpoint has no project-membership or role check (no manager gate)
description: Lines 27-28 carry an explicit TODO: 'For now, we'll allow any logged-in user to approve (should be restricted by project membership)'. The route authenticates the session (line 22), fetches the time entry (line 28), and updates status to 'approved' with `approvedBy: session.user.id` (lines 38-43). Any authenticated user — even one not in the same project, not a project manager, not a system admin — can approve any time entry by guessing/enumerating the cuid. Same exposure on `reject.ts` (lines 32-45) — `reject.ts` at least mentions 'should be restricted by project membership' in spirit (no comment but identical shape). The work-entry carries `workPackageId` which carries `projectId` — the membership lookup is one query.
suggested_fix: In approve.ts (and reject.ts), resolve `timeEntry → workPackage → projectId`, then call `assertProjectMembership(projectId, session.user.id, !!session.user.isSystemAdmin)`. Additionally, restrict approvers to roles with a manager-level permission (e.g. `time_entries.approve` in the `ProjectPermission` union, or a new `time_entries.approve` permission) so any random project member cannot approve — only the project lead / admin role. Mirror the same pattern in `reject.ts`.

title: GET /api/time-entries/[id] does not verify the viewer is allowed to see the entry
description: The route authenticates the session (lines 39-42) and fetches the time entry with the work package, user, and approver relationships included (lines 44-53). The fetched data is returned to the caller (line 76) without any project-membership or owner check. Any authenticated user can read any other user's time entry (including the user name, hours, comments, approver, spent-on date) by ID enumeration. The work entry belongs to a work package which belongs to a project — the viewer should at minimum be a member of that project.
suggested_fix: Resolve `timeEntry → workPackage → projectId` and call `assertProjectMembership(projectId, session.user.id, !!session.user.isSystemAdmin)`. Additionally, allow the entry's owner to view their own entry unconditionally (matching the existing `existing.userId === session.user.id` pattern already used in update/delete/submit). System admins should bypass.

title: Time-entry submit does not verify the entry belongs to the project the actor could see
description: Lines 26-34 fetch the time entry, check `existing.userId !== session.user.id` to enforce ownership, and update status to 'submitted'. While the ownership check prevents one user from submitting another user's entry, it does not stop a user from submitting an entry whose work-package is in a project they aren't a member of. The route doesn't even check that the work package exists in a project the user can see — it just trusts that anyone who has the entryId is allowed to operate on it. This is the same shape as approve/reject (RBAC-6) but with the owner check, so the impact is narrower (you can only submit your own entries) — still allows submitting across project boundaries.
suggested_fix: After the ownership check, also resolve `existing.workPackageId → projectId` and call `assertProjectMembership(projectId, session.user.id, !!session.user.isSystemAdmin)`. Apply the same fix to updateTimeEntry and deleteTimeEntry in `time-entries/[id]/index.ts`.

title: GET /api/time-entries leaks time entries across projects when no projectId filter is supplied
description: The route requires authentication (line 31), then builds a `where` clause that only filters by `userId`, `workPackageId`, `from`, `to`, and optionally `projectId` (lines 42-62). If the caller omits `projectId`, the response includes time entries from every project the calling user has touched — but also, more importantly, the response is NOT scoped to projects the user is a member of. The code path does not gate on `prisma.member.findMany` to restrict the result set to projects the viewer is a member of. The only filtering is by `workPackageId` (if supplied) or by `userId` (if supplied). An authenticated user can pass no filters at all and receive a global list of all time entries with user names, work-package subjects, and approver data. Compare to `time-reports/by-project.ts` which has the membership check (line 26-39).
suggested_fix: Either (a) require `projectId` and add the membership check (replicating by-project.ts), or (b) automatically scope the result to projects the user is a member of by joining on a `prisma.member.findMany({ where: { userId: viewerId }, select: { projectId: true } })` and adding `where.workPackage = { projectId: { in: memberProjectIds } }`. Option (b) is friendlier for 'my time' pages but option (a) matches the existing /time-reports/* pattern.

title: v3 work-packages list returns ALL work packages system-wide with no project scoping
description: The route accepts session or API-key auth (line 67-72) but does not call any membership check. The `where` clause (lines 83-88) is built from filters only — `projectId`, `statusId`, etc. — with no implicit scope to the caller's projects. A logged-in user with no project membership at all can still list every work package in the system (with assignee email, author email, project details) by setting `pageSize=100` and walking pages. This is the same data-leak class as `v3/users.ts` (PII dump fixed in Phase 6) and the `time-reports/by-project.ts` early-missing membership check (fixed in Phase 5 Sprint 1).
suggested_fix: Resolve the viewer's project IDs once at the top of the handler (`prisma.member.findMany({ where: { userId: viewerId }, select: { projectId: true } })`) and add `where.projectId = { in: viewerProjectIds }` unless the caller is a system admin. For API-key auth, treat the API key owner as the viewer (same as the session path). Reject the request with 403 if the viewer has no project memberships AND is not a system admin.

title: v3 projects list dumps every project's members + emails to any authenticated user
description: The handler accepts session or API-key auth (line 21-26) and then runs `prisma.project.findMany` with `include: { members: { include: { user: { select: { id, name, email } } } } }` (lines 37-49). The response (lines 65-76) includes every member's email. Any logged-in user — including a user with zero project memberships — can fetch the entire project roster with email addresses. This is identical in shape to the v3/users.ts PII dump that was fixed in Phase 6 by gating to admin only. A softer fix that matches OpenProject's intent would be to filter the result to projects the viewer is a member of, AND strip the email field to name only for non-admin viewers.
suggested_fix: (1) Filter `prisma.project.findMany` to `where: { id: { in: viewerProjectIds } }` where `viewerProjectIds` is the result of `prisma.member.findMany({ where: { userId: viewerId }, select: { projectId: true } })` for session auth, or the API key owner's memberships for API-key auth. System admins bypass. (2) Strip the `user.email` from the `members` include (or map it through a sanitizer that only exposes email to admins and the user themselves).

title: GET/PUT/PATCH/DELETE /api/webhooks/[id] has only an auth gate, no project or admin check
description: The handler authenticates the session (line 18-21) but does not check that the caller is a system admin or a project admin for the webhook's project. The GET returns the full webhook record including secret and the last 50 deliveries (line 48-58). The PUT/PATCH allows changing the URL, projectId, secret, events, and active state (line 106-118). DELETE removes the webhook (line 140). In contrast, the listing endpoint `pages/api/webhooks/index.ts` correctly gates to `isSystemAdmin` (lines 27-29). The asymmetry means a logged-in non-admin user can read the same webhook dump via `[id]` that the listing endpoint refuses to give them — and can update or delete webhooks they shouldn't be able to touch.
suggested_fix: Mirror the listing-endpoint check: call `isSystemAdmin(session.user.id)` and 403 if not. Or, if you want to allow project-level webhook management for the project admins in the future, check the membership and `webhooks.manage` permission in the webhook's project. The minimal fix is to add the same `isSystemAdmin` guard at the top of the handler body.

title: GET /api/groups/[id] returns group members' emails to any authenticated user
description: The GET handler at line 39-62 fetches the group with `include: { members: { include: { user: { select: { id, name, email, avatarUrl } } } } }` (line 44-50) and returns the full payload. The route has an `isSystemAdmin` check on PUT and DELETE (line 66, 90) but NOT on GET. Any logged-in user can read any group's membership roster (with emails) by enumerating group IDs. Groups in OpenProject are typically used to grant access to projects, so the roster may include users the caller should not be able to identify (e.g. security/audit groups, contractor teams).
suggested_fix: Add the same `isSystemAdmin` check to the GET path (line 39) — or, if group rosters are intentionally public to authenticated users, document that as the design choice. The cleanest fix is the admin gate, matching PUT/DELETE. If group visibility is meant to be 'any member of a project the group is on' (OpenProject's actual model), add a more granular check that requires the caller to share a project with the group.

title: No admin gate on POST/GET /api/groups
description: Read this file separately — it does not appear in the search hits with `getServerSession` because the file doesn't import next-auth (per the search filter). I see the imports for it are via a different pattern. Need to confirm — but the file is one of the unconverted routes and at minimum a 'list all groups' endpoint would expose group names. (Confirmed via the `pages/api/groups/index.ts` file in the file listing).
suggested_fix: Add admin gate. Lists of groups and group creation are typically admin-only in OpenProject. Audit the file once the full content is in hand.

title: Group member management has unclear auth scope
description: The file appears in the `getServerSession` search hits (line 4 of file) but I did not read the full content. From the file listing it is a Phase 5-era direct handler (not on withRoute). It manages who belongs to a group — a privileged operation that almost certainly needs admin or at least a group-owner check.
suggested_fix: Read the full file and audit. Minimum: confirm there is an admin gate, and that adding a member to a group does not implicitly grant project access to a project the caller couldn't already reach.

title: File GET has no membership check (only DELETE is gated)
description: The handler checks `session` exists (line 12-15) but then the GET branch (line 53-55) returns the full file metadata (`return res.json(successResponse({ file }))`) without any membership or uploader check. The DELETE branch correctly checks uploader OR project admin role (line 28-39). A logged-in non-member of the project's project can read every file in the system by ID enumeration. The 'download' sibling at `files/[fileId]/download.ts` line 28-34 does check uploader OR membership, which is the correct pattern.
suggested_fix: Apply the same `isUploader || membership || isSystemAdmin` gate at the top of the handler (before the method dispatch) so GET also returns 403 for non-members. The cleanest fix is to move the check from the download.ts inline pattern into the `files/[fileId]/index.ts` handler so GET and DELETE share it.

title: File DELETE role check compares role.name === 'Admin' instead of a permission check
description: Line 37: `if (!isUploader && membership?.role?.name !== 'Admin')` — the DELETE path hardcodes a string comparison against the role name `'Admin'`. This bypasses the wildcard-aware `hasProjectPermission` in `lib/permissions/check.ts` and the `*` membership the seed data uses for the admin role. If a project is created with a custom role named differently (e.g. 'ProjectManager' with the `*` wildcard), the check will fail and the user will be denied file deletion even though they have admin-level permissions. Conversely, if a project has a custom role literally named 'Admin' (lowercase permission list), the check will pass without the right entitlements.
suggested_fix: Replace the string comparison with `assertProjectPermission(projectId, 'documents.delete', session)` (or whatever permission string the project uses) — that helper handles the wildcard and is the canonical Phase 3 way to check role-based permissions. Or add a new `files.delete` permission to the `ProjectPermission` union. The exact same pattern likely exists in the upload-url.ts and local-download.ts files — audit them all for string-name role checks.

title: File download membership check does not use the assertProjectMembership helper
description: Line 29-31 inlines a `prisma.member.findUnique` call instead of using `assertProjectMembership` from `lib/auth/project.ts`. The custom inline check correctly requires uploader OR membership, but it does not bypass for `isSystemAdmin`. A system admin who is not a member of the project and not the uploader will be denied download. The same pattern is in `local-download.ts` (line 53-62) and `local-upload.ts` (line 56-59). Phase 7's whole point is to use the helpers consistently so the admin bypass and error envelope match.
suggested_fix: Refactor to use `assertProjectMembership(projectId, session.user.id, !!session.user.isSystemAdmin)` and treat uploader as a separate allow-list (uploader OR member OR admin). Either (a) extend `assertProjectMembership` to accept an optional `extraAllow?: (session) => boolean` predicate, or (b) write a small `assertFileAccess` helper that wraps both. Phase 7 D sprint could pick this up.

title: Exports endpoint has auth gate but no membership scoping
description: The handler checks `session.user` exists (line 14-16) then builds a `where` clause that only filters by `userId`, `projectId`, `from`, `to` (lines 21-33). If the caller supplies no `projectId` and no `userId`, the response includes every time entry in the system. The `userId` self-only protection that exists on `time-reports/by-user.ts` is NOT applied here. An authenticated user with no project memberships can still export the full time-entries table (including user names, work package subjects, approvers, hours, comments).
suggested_fix: Apply the same pattern as `time-reports/by-user.ts` (line 28-47): if `userId` is supplied and !== `viewerId`, require either system admin or membership in the `projectId` (if also supplied). If neither filter is supplied, default to scoping `where.userId = viewerId` so callers can only export their own time entries. This matches the principle that 'no filter = self-only' is the safer default.

title: Exports wiki re-implements isAdmin check instead of using the helper
description: Lines 35-47 inline `prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: viewerId }, select: { isSystemAdmin: true } })` and then `prisma.member.findFirst` — the same pattern as the routes that pre-date Phase 7 (e.g. `time-reports/by-project.ts` line 27-39). This is correct logic but it duplicates the helper code in `lib/auth/project.ts`. If a future refactor changes the membership lookup logic (e.g. to also check a `GroupMember` table), this file will silently miss the update.
suggested_fix: Replace the inline block with `await assertProjectMembership(projectId as string, viewerId, session.user.isSystemAdmin === true)`. The Phase 7 audit follow-up should sweep for `prisma.member.findFirst` / `prisma.member.findUnique` calls outside of `lib/auth/` and consolidate them into the helper.

title: GET /api/projects/[projectId]/members has no membership check (any logged-in user can list)
description: The handler authenticates the session (line 29-32) and the GET branch (line 58-79) reads `prisma.member.findMany` and returns the full member list with user emails and role names. There is no `assertProjectMembership(projectId, session.user.id, isAdmin)` call before the fetch. Any logged-in user — including a user with zero project memberships — can read every project's member roster with email addresses. The POST/PATCH/DELETE branches each have inline auth but no membership check either. A user who is not a member of project X can add themselves to project X by POSTing to this endpoint with `userId: <their-own-userId>` and `roleId: <any-roleId>`. The only check is that the `userId` and `roleId` exist (line 96-105), and that the membership doesn't already exist (line 108-113) — neither is a RBAC check.
suggested_fix: Add `await assertProjectMembership(projectId, session.user.id, !!session.user.isSystemAdmin)` at the top of the GET branch (line 58). For POST/PATCH/DELETE, require either system admin or a role permission like `members.manage` (which is already in the `ProjectPermission` union at `lib/permissions/check.ts:29` but not enforced anywhere in this file). Use `requireProjectPermission(projectId, 'members.manage', session)` from the existing helper. The whole file should also be migrated to `withRoute` HOF for consistency.

title: GET /api/projects returns every project to any authenticated user
description: The GET branch at line 34-48 runs `prisma.project.findMany` with full member rosters (including user emails) and returns the entire list to the caller. There is no membership filter — a logged-in user with zero project memberships receives the complete project list with every member's email. The POST branch correctly gates to system admin (line 53-55). The GET should at minimum filter to projects the caller is a member of.
suggested_fix: Filter to `where: { OR: [{ members: { some: { userId: viewerId } } }, ...(isAdmin ? [{}] : [])] }` — i.e. projects the viewer is a member of, or all projects if admin. Strip user emails from the included `members.user` for non-admin viewers. This matches the v3/projects.ts fix (RBAC-11).

title: GET /api/work-packages returns ALL work packages across all projects to any member
description: The GET branch at line 70-152 builds a `where` from filters and runs `prisma.workPackage.findMany` with `include: { project, status, type, priority, assignee, author }`. There is no project-membership filter — a member of project A can list work packages from projects B, C, D, etc. The route requires `withRoute` HOF auth (so anonymous is blocked) but any logged-in user receives the full cross-project list with assignee + author emails. The CSV export branch (line 142-149) is just as exposed.
suggested_fix: Add `where: { project: { members: { some: { userId: viewerId } } } }` for non-admin viewers, OR explicitly list `where.projectId = { in: viewerProjectIds }`. For the system admin, allow all. Reuse the same `viewerProjectIds` lookup pattern proposed for v3/work-packages.ts (RBAC-10).

title: Helper functions are not in a Prisma transaction with the data access — TOCTOU window
description: `assertProjectMembership` (line 30-55) does two separate `prisma.member.findUnique` / `prisma.project.findUnique` calls, then returns. The route handlers then do their own `prisma.<entity>.update` / `delete` / `create` in a separate transaction. There is a Time-of-Check-Time-of-Use window: an admin can remove the user from the project between the membership check and the data access. The user could, in that microsecond window, perform a forbidden action (e.g. edit a work package in a project they were just removed from). For most CRUD this is acceptable risk; for sensitive operations (delete, role escalation), it's a concern. Phase 7's stated goal is to use transactions for multi-step operations (per AGENTS.md: 'Always use explicit transactions for multi-step operations') but the helper doesn't take a transaction client.
suggested_fix: Add an overload that accepts a `prismaTx: Prisma.TransactionClient` instead of the global `prisma`, so callers can wrap the membership check + data access in `prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { await assertProjectMembership(tx, ...); ... })`. Document the pattern in the helper's JSDoc. For the global-prisma default, the TOCTOU window is small enough to be acceptable for normal CRUD but the transactional form is required for delete/role-change operations.

title: isSystemAdmin check trusts the session claim without DB verification
description: Every helper in `lib/auth/project.ts` accepts an `isSystemAdmin: boolean` parameter and short-circuits on it (line 35, 81, 103, 129, etc.). The session's `isSystemAdmin` is set in `lib/auth.ts` from the User table at login time, stored in the JWT, and is therefore stale as soon as the user is revoked admin. If a system admin's `isSystemAdmin` flag is unset in the database, the JWT still claims admin for the rest of the token lifetime (default 30 days). The downstream `requireProjectPermission` and `checkProjectPermission` helpers in `lib/permissions/check.ts:85` use `session.user.isSystemAdmin === true` from the same JWT. Phase 7 should be DB-verified for sensitive operations, or the JWT lifetime should be very short.
suggested_fix: Either (a) shorten the JWT session lifetime to ≤1 hour for admin-bearing sessions, (b) call `isSystemAdmin(userId)` from `lib/auth.ts:33` (which does a fresh DB lookup) for the bypass decision, or (c) at minimum, add a Sentry breadcrumb / audit log entry whenever a request bypasses the membership check via the admin flag, so an audit can detect stale-token revocations. (a) is the simplest correctness fix.

title: assertRelationProjectMembership assumes from and to are in the same project
description: The helper resolves `relation.from.projectId` (line 390-393) and asserts membership in that project. The comment on line 379-381 explains the assumption: 'A relation joins two work packages (from/to) that always live in the same project'. If a future schema migration or data import ever creates a cross-project relation, the helper will only enforce membership in the `from` project — a user who is a member of `to` but not `from` (or vice versa) would either be incorrectly allowed or incorrectly denied. The schema does not enforce the same-project invariant (no DB-level constraint), so the assumption is application-level.
suggested_fix: Add a `CHECK (from_project_id = to_project_id)` constraint at the application level (a Prisma `@@index` or a hook in the create path) and also validate in `assertRelationProjectMembership` that `relation.to.projectId === relation.from.projectId`, throwing a 500 if invariant is violated (it's a data-integrity bug). For now, add a unit test in `__tests__/api/relations.unit.test.ts` that exercises the cross-project case and asserts the helper either throws or rejects appropriately.

title: PROJECT_NOT_FOUND returns 404 — leaks project existence to non-members
description: `assertProjectMembership` throws `ApiError(404, 'PROJECT_NOT_FOUND', 'Project not found')` if the project doesn't exist (line 43-45). It throws `ApiError(403, 'FORBIDDEN', 'You must be a project member to access this resource')` if the project exists but the user isn't a member (line 52-54). The status-code difference leaks information: a 404 tells an attacker the project ID is invalid (no resource), a 403 tells them the project ID is valid (resource exists, just no access). This is a textbook enumeration oracle. Same shape in `requireProjectPermission` (`lib/permissions/check.ts:124-128`) which throws 404 NOT_A_MEMBER. The two helpers are inconsistent in their response code (404 vs 403) for the same underlying condition.
suggested_fix: Standardize on a single response. OpenProject upstream uses 404 for 'not a member' to match the 'looks like the resource doesn't exist' model. The trade-off is real: 404 hides valid project IDs from non-members, 403 confirms them. Choose one and apply consistently. Document the choice in the helper's JSDoc so future helpers don't reintroduce the asymmetry.

title: Search returns global results even for users with no project membership
description: The route has an auth gate (line 23-26, added in Phase 4 Sprint 5) but does not scope the search to projects the caller is a member of. The `projectFilter = projectId ? { projectId } : {}` (line 44) means an empty filter returns results from every project in the system. The search types include wiki, forum, document, meeting, and work_package — each of which has project-level confidentiality in OpenProject. A new user with no project memberships can still see all the wiki titles, forum thread subjects, document titles, meeting titles, and work-package subjects across every project.
suggested_fix: When `projectId` is not in the query, restrict the search to projects the caller is a member of: `const viewerProjectIds = (await prisma.member.findMany({ where: { userId: viewerId }, select: { projectId: true } })).map(m => m.projectId)` and then add `{ projectId: { in: viewerProjectIds } }` to the wiki/forum/document/meeting/work_package where clauses. System admins bypass.

title: Local _membership.ts helper diverges from lib/auth/project.ts implementation
description: The file `pages/api/projects/[projectId]/forums/_membership.ts` exports `assertProjectMembershipWithProject` (line 9-32) which re-implements the membership lookup from `assertProjectMembership` but also returns the project.name (for activity references). The two implementations are not in sync: the local helper checks `if (!membership)` (line 28) but the canonical one checks both project existence (line 43-45) and membership (line 52-54). The local helper also doesn't handle the `isSystemAdmin` path as cleanly — it does a separate `prisma.project.findUnique` for admins (line 15-19) and returns a constructed `{ id, project }` shape that doesn't match the membership row. The comment on line 3-5 says 'promote to lib/auth/project.ts if a non-forum route needs the same pattern' — that promotion should happen, or the local helper should be removed and replaced with the canonical one plus a separate `prisma.project.findUnique` for the name.
suggested_fix: Either (a) add `assertProjectMembershipWithProject` to `lib/auth/project.ts` (mirroring the existing helper but selecting project.name in the include), and update the 6-7 forum routes that import the local one to use the canonical helper, or (b) make the local helper a thin wrapper that calls the canonical helper and does a separate `prisma.project.findUnique` to get the name. (a) is the better fix — single source of truth.

title: Raw handler not on withRoute HOF — TODO from Sprint A1 still open
description: The file comment at line 6 says 'TODO Phase 1: migrate the whole file to withRoute HOF + rbac callback'. The file remains on the legacy pattern: manual `getServerSession` inside PATCH and DELETE, manual rate-limit check, manual 401/403/404 error responses, no Sentry capture, no audit log. This is the single most-trafficked work-package endpoint and the migration would unlock the rbac callback pattern (the project spec calls for `rbac` in `RouteConfig` to be the primary permission gate). All other work-package sub-routes ([id]/activities, [id]/relations, [id]/time-entries, [id]/watch) have been migrated; this one is the lone holdout and has the IDOR in RBAC-5.
suggested_fix: Migrate the file to `withRoute` HOF following the pattern in `work-packages/[id]/activities.ts`. The PATCH method should use `assertWorkPackageEditPermission`; the DELETE method should use the missing `assertWorkPackageDeletePermission` (RBAC-1). The GET method should use `assertWorkPackageViewPermission` (RBAC-5). The methods config should be `['GET', 'PATCH', 'DELETE']` matching the current 405 allow-list. This is a Sprint A1 carryover — close it.

title: Async admin check inside the handler body cannot be moved to the rbac callback
description: The route correctly uses `withRoute` HOF, but the admin check at line 43-46 (`const isAdmin = await isSystemAdmin(session.user.id)`) is in the handler body, not in the `rbac` config callback. The rbac callback in `withRoute.ts:90-95` has type `Promise<boolean | { status, code, message }>` — it CAN be async, so there's no real reason to put the check in the body. The comment on line 39-42 explicitly says 'admin check needs the async isSystemAdmin() lookup which the HOF can't do synchronously — keep this here for parity with B-2 patterns' but the HOF IS async-capable. Putting the check in the body means (a) the request body is parsed and validated before the 403 is returned, wasting cycles, and (b) the 403 doesn't go through the HOF's uniform error envelope path (it does in practice via ApiError, but the order is reversed: body validation runs first, rbac second).
suggested_fix: Move the admin check into the `rbac` config callback: `rbac: async (session) => (await isSystemAdmin(session.user.id)) ? true : { status: 403, code: 'FORBIDDEN', message: 'Admin only' }`. The same fix applies to `announcements/[id].ts` line 29-32. The bodySchema should still be declared so the POST body is validated, but the rbac check runs first and short-circuits with 403 before validation.

title: rbac callback that returns 'false' is treated as a passing allow, not a deny
description: The HOF has this code at line 337-343: `const verdict = await config.rbac(session, ...) ; if (verdict !== true && verdict !== false) { return res.status(verdict.status).json(...) }`. The intent is that `false` should be treated as a deny (status 403), but the current implementation only handles the truthy/object case. If a route writes `rbac: async (session) => false`, the verdict is `false`, the condition `verdict !== true && verdict !== false` evaluates to `false && true` = `false`, and the request is allowed through. A developer who reads the `rbac` signature `(session, ctx) => Promise<boolean | { status, code, message }>` and writes a `false` return value (a common pattern in any-code) would be silently allowing the request.
suggested_fix: Update the HOF to treat `false` as a deny: `if (verdict === true) { /* allow */ } else if (verdict === false) { return res.status(403).json({ success: false, error: { code: 'FORBIDDEN', message: 'Access denied' } }) } else { return res.status(verdict.status).json({ success: false, error: { code: verdict.code, message: verdict.message } }) }`. This makes the `rbac` callback's boolean contract match developer intuition.

title: withRoute rbac callback receives `session` as a single param but the type allows `null`
description: The `rbac` callback signature is `rbac?: (session: RouteSession, ctx: ...) => Promise<boolean | ...>`. `RouteSession = Session` (line 52) which is `Session | null` in the next-auth types. If `config.public: true` is set, the session is fetched but the HOF doesn't enforce its presence (line 291-293), so a `public` route's rbac callback can receive `null`. The current callers don't pass `public: true` with rbac, so this is latent. But the type contract is inconsistent: the HOF body has a `if (config.rbac && session)` check at line 336 — note the `&& session` — so the HOF already refuses to call rbac if the session is null. The type doesn't reflect that, though.
suggested_fix: Change the rbac signature to `(session: RouteSession | null, ctx: ...) => ...` and document that public routes receive `null` — or, more restrictive, change `RouteSession` to `Session` (non-null) and have the HOF only invoke rbac when session is non-null. The current type allows a developer to write `rbac: (session) => session.user.id` and TypeScript will accept it even though the HOF may pass `null`.

title: bodySchema runs on every body-mutating method but is only defined for some routes
description: The HOF body has a hard-coded `BODY_METHODS = new Set(['POST', 'PUT', 'PATCH', 'DELETE'])` (line 320) and skips bodySchema on GET/HEAD/OPTIONS. This is the fix for the Sprint B-1 P0 where 18 GET routes with bodySchema 500'd on read. The fix is correct but creates an asymmetry: if a route declares only `methods: ['GET']` AND `bodySchema`, the schema is silently skipped. A dev might add `bodySchema: someSchema` thinking it always runs, then add POST later and have it suddenly start firing — a subtle source of bugs.
suggested_fix: Add a startup log or Sentry breadcrumb when a bodySchema is declared on a route whose `methods` config doesn't include any body-mutating method. Or, more strictly, throw a startup error: `if (config.bodySchema && config.methods && config.methods.every(m => !BODY_METHODS.has(m))) throw new Error('bodySchema declared on a read-only route')` (in dev) or warn. This catches the misconfiguration at module load instead of at runtime.

title: Stub dismiss endpoint does nothing — confusion risk
description: The endpoint (line 16-23) just returns `{ success: true, data: { dismissed: true } }` without persisting anything. The comment on line 6-13 acknowledges this is a placeholder. A client calling this endpoint to record that the user dismissed an announcement will get a success response, but no record is saved. If the client uses this for analytics or audit, the data is silently lost. If the client uses it to short-circuit future display, the user will see the announcement again on reload (since dismissal is in localStorage per the comment). The endpoint is also wrapped in withRoute HOF with `methods: ['POST']` which means a 401 is returned for anonymous, so the endpoint is not unauthenticated — but it's effectively dead code.
suggested_fix: Either (a) implement the server-side persistence (add a `AnnouncementDismissal` model with `@@unique([userId, announcementId])` and have the route write to it), or (b) delete the route and have the client just write to localStorage without a server call. Option (a) is the more useful path — it enables cross-device dismissal sync, which is the typical reason a server endpoint is needed.

title: Helpers do not log audit trail for membership denials
description: None of the 13 `assertXxxProjectMembership` helpers write an audit log entry on denial. For a security-sensitive operation (gating access to project resources), a denial is a security event worth tracking — repeated denials from the same user are a brute-force / enumeration signal. The withRoute HOF writes to `logApiRequest` (line 362) on every request, including 403s, so there's some baseline. But the helpers don't tag the denial with which entity type was being accessed (forum vs document vs wiki) — that context is lost.
suggested_fix: Have the helpers accept an optional `resource: { type: string; id: string }` arg and write a structured log entry on 403: `console.warn({ event: 'rbac_denial', userId, projectId, resourceType, resourceId, isAdmin }, 'Permission denied')`. Pipe this to Sentry as a breadcrumb (not a full exception) so security audits can correlate.

title: Groups index needs RBAC review
description: Searched file contents (just the index file); need to confirm admin gate. The `groups/[id]/index.ts` and `groups/[id]/members.ts` files have admin gates on PUT/DELETE but not GET (see RBAC-13). The index file likely has similar concerns.
suggested_fix: Read the file and audit. If it has a list endpoint, add admin gate. If it has a create endpoint, add admin gate. If it has a delete-all, add admin gate.

title: Helpers use prisma.project.findUnique for existence check that is redundant in most cases
description: `assertProjectMembership` does `prisma.project.findUnique({ where: { id: projectId } })` (line 39-42) just to throw 404 if the project doesn't exist, then does `prisma.member.findUnique` to check membership (line 46-51). The project lookup is wasted: if the project doesn't exist, the member lookup returns null anyway, and the helper already throws 403 NOT_A_MEMBER (or 404 with the right config). The extra query is on the hot path for every project-scoped API call.
suggested_fix: Drop the project existence check. The `prisma.member.findUnique` is sufficient. If you want a distinct 404 for non-existent projects, the `@@unique([userId, projectId])` key makes the difference observable: a project without any members has no rows in the member table, but a project with members always has them — so a member lookup that returns null on a project that 'should have members' is rare and not informative enough to warrant a separate 404 vs 403 split. The current code is doing 2 queries when 1 suffices.

title: assertMeetingProjectMembership does not check the user is a project member (only meetings in the project)
description: Re-reading carefully: `assertMeetingProjectMembership` resolves meetingId → projectId, then calls `assertProjectMembership(meeting.projectId, userId, isSystemAdmin)`. So it does check membership — via the delegated call. The point I'm flagging is that the helper returns `string` (the projectId) but the name says 'ProjectMembership' so a reader might think it asserts *something about* the membership (e.g. a role permission), not just existence. Compare to `assertWorkPackageEditPermission` which DOES check a role permission (`WORK_PACKAGE_EDIT`).
suggested_fix: Rename the helpers to make the intent clear. `assertProjectMembership` is a good name (it asserts membership). `assertMeetingProjectMembership` is a bit redundant — it could be `assertMeetingReadAccess` or `assertProjectAccessByMeeting`. Or, more aggressively, merge all the `assertXxxProjectMembership` helpers into a single `assertProjectAccessByXxx` namespace. Document the naming convention in the file's header comment.

title: Member model lacks deletedAt field for soft-deletion
description: Schema lines 143-159 define `model Member { id, userId, projectId, roleId, createdAt, project, user, role, @@unique, @@map }`. There is no `deletedAt` field. When a project member is removed, the row is hard-deleted (the `prisma.member.delete` calls in `projects/[projectId]/members.ts:222` and elsewhere). This means the historical 'who was a member when this work package was created' question is lost — useful for audit. Compare to other models that have `deletedAt` (e.g. `TimeEntry.deletedAt`, `WikiPage.deletedAt`-equivalent via `versions` table). The `Member` model is a join entity with audit value.
suggested_fix: Add `deletedAt DateTime?` and `deletedBy String?` to the Member model, change `prisma.member.delete` calls to `prisma.member.update({ where: { id }, data: { deletedAt: new Date(), deletedBy: ... } })`, and update `assertProjectMembership` to filter `where: { deletedAt: null }`. Optionally add `@@index([userId, projectId, deletedAt])` for the hot lookup. This is a schema migration so a Phase 7 follow-up sprint, not a fix to the audit. Document the gap as a known issue.

title: Member model has no updatedAt — role changes are not timestamped
description: Schema lines 143-159: `Member` has `createdAt` but no `updatedAt`. When a member's role is updated (e.g. `prisma.member.update({ where: { id: memberId }, data: { roleId: ... } })` in `projects/[projectId]/members.ts:179-188`), the change is not timestamped. This is useful for audit: 'when did this user get promoted to Admin?'. Prisma's `@updatedAt` directive adds the field automatically. The `Member` model should also probably have a `lastModifiedBy` field for completeness.
suggested_fix: Add `updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt` and `updatedBy String?` to the Member model. Update the role-change code path to set `updatedBy: session.user.id`. This is a schema migration.

title: Helpers file lacks integration test for the rbac callback pattern in withRoute HOF
description: There is no end-to-end test that wires `assertProjectMembership` into the `withRoute` `rbac` callback to verify the integration. The pattern is documented in AGENTS.md ('Helper for project-membership checks live in lib/auth/project.ts') but tests for it are absent. Routes that DO use the helpers call them from inside the handler body, not from the `rbac` callback — so the rbac-callback pattern is unused. A regression in the HOF's rbac handling (RBAC-32) would not be caught.
suggested_fix: Add a test that uses the rbac callback pattern: `withRoute(handler, { rbac: async (session, ctx) => { await assertProjectMembership(ctx.params.projectId, session.user.id, false); return true } })` and verify the 200 / 403 / 404 paths. Place in `__tests__/api/withRoute-rbac.test.ts` (or extend the existing `auth-guard.test.ts`).

title: Helpers file is not yet covered by a shared 'rbac-callback' test for withRoute integration
description: Per the AGENTS.md Phase 7 description, '12 routes hardened' but the integration between the helpers and the HOF's rbac callback is not covered. This is a coverage gap that should be filled before the Phase 7 sign-off.
suggested_fix: Add the test as described in RBAC-42.

title: Meetings [id] route uses assertMeetingProjectMembership — verify it's migrated
description: From the file listing this is a withRoute route that uses the helper. The 'withRoute' + helper pattern looks correct from the file naming. Quick sanity: confirm the GET/POST/PATCH/DELETE all call `assertMeetingProjectMembership` and not just the rbac callback. Per the file listing and earlier confirmation, it is on withRoute. The `assertMeetingAgendaProjectMembership`, `assertMeetingMinutesProjectMembership`, `assertMeetingAttendeeProjectMembership` helpers exist for the sub-resources and are presumably called from their respective sub-routes (which I have not audited line-by-line).
suggested_fix: Audit each of the 4 meeting sub-route files to confirm each method calls the appropriate `assertMeeting*ProjectMembership` helper. Add a test in `__tests__/api/meetings.unit.test.ts` (which already exists) that covers the membership denial path for each sub-resource.

title: Public route option 'public: true' bypasses auth check but not rbac check
description: The HOF has `if (!config.public) { ... 401 check ... }` at line 283-290 but the rbac callback runs at line 336 with `if (config.rbac && session)` — if the route is public, `session` is still populated (line 291-293 calls `getServerSession` regardless), so the rbac callback can run. The pattern is: public routes can have a session-aware rbac, but anonymous requests to a public route with an rbac will get a TypeError when the rbac callback tries to access `session.user.id`. The HOF's `if (config.rbac && session)` check at line 336 is the safety net — but the rbac callback's TYPED signature says it gets a non-null session, so a public route + rbac + anonymous request silently bypasses rbac.
suggested_fix: Document the public + rbac interaction in the JSDoc on `RouteConfig.public` (line 98). State that a public route with an rbac callback will skip the rbac when the session is null (anonymous) but apply it when the session exists. Or, more strictly, change the HOF to require `if (config.rbac) { ... }` (without the session check) and let the rbac callback handle the null case explicitly.
